title: Low level design for a Parking Lot system. Narrate the classes, show a class diagram and a sequence diagram, use design

transcript.text: Low level design for a Parking Lot system. Narrate the classes, show a class diagram and a sequence diagram, use design patterns, and write + run the code.

blocks.md:
# Parking Lot — Low Level Design

> 💬 *"Before I jump into classes, let me restate the problem and lock down scope with a few clarifying questions, then I'll work through entities, a class diagram, the patterns I'll use, and finally code it up and run it."*

## 1. Requirements & Clarifications

**Problem:** Design the software that runs a multi-level parking lot — vehicles enter, get assigned a spot, park, and on exit pay based on duration.

**Clarifying questions I'd ask the interviewer:**
- **Scale/shape:** Multiple floors? Multiple entry/exit gates? (Assume: yes — N floors, multiple gates.)
- **Vehicle types & spot types:** Just cars, or motorcycle/car/truck? Can a small vehicle take a larger spot if its own size is full? (Assume: 3 types — Motorcycle, Car, Truck — and a vehicle may "fit up" into a larger free spot.)
- **Pricing:** Flat rate or per-hour by vehicle type? (Assume: per-hour, type-dependent — and pluggable so it can change.)
- **Payment:** Cash/card/UPI? Do we model payment processing? (Assume: model a Payment with pluggable methods, simplified.)
- **Spot assignment policy:** Nearest-to-entrance, random, or first-free? (Assume: pluggable strategy; default = nearest free compatible spot.)
- **Ticketing:** Ticket on entry, pay on exit? Lost ticket handling? (Assume: ticket on entry, pay-on-exit; out of scope: lost ticket.)
- **Out of scope (state explicitly):** reservations, monthly passes, license-plate OCR, persistence/DB, distributed multi-lot.

**Functional requirements (in scope):**
1. Issue a ticket and assign a compatible spot when a vehicle enters; reject if full.
2. Free the spot and compute a fee on exit; take payment.
3. Support multiple floors, multiple vehicle/spot types, fit-up.
4. Pluggable **pricing** and **spot-assignment** strategies.
5. Real-time availability count per floor/type.

**Non-functional:** thread-safe (many gates act concurrently), no double-allocation of a spot, every entry reaches a defined outcome (ticket **or** explicit rejection), extensible.

## 2. Use Cases & Actors

**Actors:** Driver (enters/exits), Entry Gate, Exit Gate, Admin (configures floors/rates), Payment provider.

| Use case | Actor | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Enter & park | Driver via Entry Gate | Ticket issued + spot assigned, OR rejected "lot full" |
| Exit & pay | Driver via Exit Gate | Fee computed, payment taken, spot freed |
| Check availability | Gate/Admin | Free-spot counts by floor & type |
| Configure rates/policy | Admin | Pricing / assignment strategy swapped |

> 💬 *"The two hot paths are **enter** and **exit**. Everything else supports those, so I'll make sure both are airtight — especially that 'enter' never silently fails and never hands the same spot to two cars."*

## 3. Core Entities (Objects)

> 💬 *"I extract the nouns from the problem statement — parking lot, floor, spot, vehicle, ticket, payment, gate — those become my classes. Then verbs — assign, park, calculate fee, pay — become methods or strategies."*

- **Vehicle** (abstract) → Motorcycle / Car / Truck: has a plate and a `VehicleType` (its size).
- **ParkingSpot**: an id, a `SpotType`, occupied/free state, and which vehicle occupies it.
- **ParkingFloor**: holds spots, indexes them by type for O(1) "find a free spot."
- **ParkingLot**: top-level facade; owns floors, gates, strategies; orchestrates enter/exit. **Single source of truth** for active tickets.
- **Ticket**: the in-flight record — vehicle, spot, entry time; the truth that survives entry→exit.
- **SpotAssignmentStrategy** (interface): picks a spot for a vehicle.
- **PricingStrategy** (interface): computes fee from a ticket + exit time.
- **Payment** + **PaymentMethod**: settles the fee.

## 4. Class Design (diagram)

This class diagram shows the structure and relationships.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class VehicleType {
        <<enum>>
        MOTORCYCLE
        CAR
        TRUCK
    }
    class SpotType {
        <<enum>>
        SMALL
        MEDIUM
        LARGE
    }
    class Vehicle {
        <<abstract>>
        +str plate
        +VehicleType type
    }
    class Motorcycle
    class Car
    class Truck

    class ParkingSpot {
        +str id
        +SpotType type
        +Vehicle vehicle
        +bool is_free()
        +assign(v)
        +release()
    }
    class ParkingFloor {
        +int level
        +add_spot(s)
        +free_spots(spot_type)
        +counts()
    }
    class Ticket {
        +str id
        +Vehicle vehicle
        +ParkingSpot spot
        +float entry_ts
    }
    class SpotAssignmentStrategy {
        <<interface>>
        +find(floors, vehicle)
    }
    class NearestFirstStrategy
    class PricingStrategy {
        <<interface>>
        +price(ticket, exit_ts)
    }
    class HourlyPricing
    class Payment {
        +float amount
        +pay(method)
    }
    class PaymentMethod {
        <<interface>>
        +collect(amount)
    }
    class ParkingLot {
        +enter(vehicle) Ticket
        +exit(ticket_id, method) Payment
        +availability()
    }

    Vehicle <|-- Motorcycle
    Vehicle <|-- Car
    Vehicle <|-- Truck
    SpotAssignmentStrategy <|.. NearestFirstStrategy
    PricingStrategy <|.. HourlyPricing
    ParkingLot o-- ParkingFloor
    ParkingLot o-- SpotAssignmentStrategy
    ParkingLot o-- PricingStrategy
    ParkingLot o-- Ticket
    ParkingFloor o-- ParkingSpot
    Ticket o-- ParkingSpot
    Ticket o-- Vehicle
    ParkingSpot o-- Vehicle
```

**Relationships in words:**
- **Inheritance:** concrete vehicles extend abstract `Vehicle`; concrete strategies *implement* the strategy interfaces (dashed = realization).
- **Composition:** a `ParkingFloor` owns its `ParkingSpot`s; a `ParkingLot` owns its floors — spots don't exist without the lot.
- **Association/aggregation:** `ParkingLot` holds the strategies and the live `Ticket` registry; a `Ticket` references the `Vehicle` and the `ParkingSpot` it bound together — that reference is what keeps the request whole from entry to exit.

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

**Applied (wired in §6/§7 code):**
- **Strategy** — `SpotAssignmentStrategy` (nearest-first) and `PricingStrategy` (hourly). Both are injected into `ParkingLot`, so policy changes without touching the orchestrator. *This is the main extensibility seam.*
- **Factory Method** — `VehicleFactory.create(type, plate)` builds the right `Vehicle` subclass, so the gate doesn't `if/else` over types.
- **Facade** — `ParkingLot.enter()/exit()` give gates one simple API over floors, spots, strategies, and payment.
- **Abstract Base Class / Template** — `Vehicle`, `PaymentMethod` define contracts; concretes fill them.

**Extension points (NOT yet wired — I'd call this out honestly):** Observer for "floor full" display boards, State pattern for a richer spot lifecycle (RESERVED/OUT_OF_SERVICE). I won't claim these as implemented.

**SOLID:**
- **S** — each class has one job (spot tracks occupancy; floor indexes spots; lot orchestrates).
- **O** — new pricing or assignment = new Strategy class, lot unchanged. New vehicle = new subclass + factory line.
- **L** — any `Vehicle`/`PricingStrategy` substitutes cleanly.
- **D** — `ParkingLot` depends on strategy **interfaces**, not concretes (injected via constructor).

> 💬 *"Strategy is the heart of it — layman's terms, the parking lot doesn't hard-code **how** to pick a spot or **how** to price; you hand it a rulebook and it follows whichever rulebook you gave it. Swap the rulebook, behavior changes, no surgery on the core."*

Now I'll implement it as small modules, run it, and only show code that ran clean.

blocks.name: write_file

input.content:
"""ParkingLot facade — orchestrates enter/exit, owns the active-ticket registry."""
from __future__ import annotations
import threading
from models import Vehicle, ParkingFloor, Ticket
from strategies import (SpotAssignmentStrategy, PricingStrategy, NearestFirstStrategy,
                        HourlyPricing, Payment, PaymentMethod)


class LotFullError(Exception):
    pass


class ParkingLot:
    def __init__(self, floors: list[ParkingFloor],
                 assigner: SpotAssignmentStrategy | None = None,
                 pricer: PricingStrategy | None = None):
        self.floors = floors
        self.assigner = assigner or NearestFirstStrategy()
        self.pricer = pricer or HourlyPricing()
        self._active: dict[str, Ticket] = {}        # source of truth for in-flight cars
        self._lock = threading.RLock()              # one model: lock-guarded shared state

    def enter(self, vehicle: Vehicle, now: float) -> Ticket:
        if vehicle is None:
            raise ValueError("vehicle required")
        # select-and-assign must be atomic so two gates can't grab the same spot.
        with self._lock:
            found = self.assigner.find(self.floors, vehicle)
            if found is None:
                raise LotFullError(f"no spot for {vehicle}")
            spot, level = found
            spot.assign(vehicle)                    # commit while still holding the lock
            ticket = Ticket(vehicle, spot, level, now)
            self._active[ticket.id] = ticket
            return ticket

    def exit(self, ticket_id: str, method: PaymentMethod, now: float) -> Payment:
        with self._lock:
            ticket = self._active.get(ticket_id)
            if ticket is None:
                raise KeyError(f"unknown/used ticket {ticket_id}")
            amount = self.pricer.price(ticket, now)
            payment = Payment(amount)
            if not payment.pay(method):
                raise RuntimeError("payment failed")
            ticket.spot.release()                   # free spot only after payment ok
            del self._active[ticket_id]
            return payment

    def availability(self) -> dict[int, dict[str, int]]:
        with self._lock:
            return {f.level: f.counts() for f in self.floors}

    def active_count(self) -> int:
        with self._lock:
            return len(self._active)

blocks.output: File created successfully at: parking_lot.py (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

blocks.name: write_file

input.content:
"""Driver + assertions exercising tricky cases."""
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
from models import (VehicleType, SpotType, ParkingSpot, ParkingFloor, VehicleFactory)
from strategies import HourlyPricing, CashPayment, CardPayment
from parking_lot import ParkingLot, LotFullError


def build_lot():
    f0 = ParkingFloor(0)
    f1 = ParkingFloor(1)
    # Floor 0: 1 small, 1 medium, 1 large.  Floor 1: 2 medium.
    f0.add_spot(ParkingSpot("0-S1", SpotType.SMALL))
    f0.add_spot(ParkingSpot("0-M1", SpotType.MEDIUM))
    f0.add_spot(ParkingSpot("0-L1", SpotType.LARGE))
    f1.add_spot(ParkingSpot("1-M1", SpotType.MEDIUM))
    f1.add_spot(ParkingSpot("1-M2", SpotType.MEDIUM))
    return ParkingLot([f0, f1])


def main():
    lot = build_lot()
    car = VehicleFactory.create

    # 1) Validation: bad input rejected before mutating state.
    try:
        lot.enter(None, now=0); assert False
    except ValueError:
        print("OK  null vehicle rejected")

    # 2) Happy path: car gets nearest smallest-fitting spot (MEDIUM on floor 0).
    t = lot.enter(car(VehicleType.CAR, "KA01"), now=0)
    assert t.spot.id == "0-M1", t.spot.id
    print("OK  car parked at", t.spot.id)

    # 3) Fit-up: motorcycle takes the SMALL before climbing sizes.
    tm = lot.enter(car(VehicleType.MOTORCYCLE, "KA02"), now=0)
    assert tm.spot.id == "0-S1", tm.spot.id
    print("OK  bike parked at", tm.spot.id)

    # 4) Truck needs LARGE -> only 0-L1.
    tt = lot.enter(car(VehicleType.TRUCK, "KA03"), now=0)
    assert tt.spot.id == "0-L1"
    # Next truck: no LARGE left -> explicit rejection, not silent drop.
    try:
        lot.enter(car(VehicleType.TRUCK, "KA04"), now=0); assert False
    except LotFullError:
        print("OK  second truck rejected (no large spot)")

    # 5) Exit & pricing: car parked 2.5h -> ceil = 3h x 20 = 60.
    pay = lot.exit(t.id, CardPayment(), now=2.5 * 3600)
    assert pay.amount == 60.0 and pay.paid, pay.amount
    print("OK  car billed", pay.amount)

    # 6) Double-exit / unknown ticket rejected.
    try:
        lot.exit(t.id, CashPayment(), now=0); assert False
    except KeyError:
        print("OK  reused ticket rejected")

    # 7) Concurrency: 20 cars race for limited MEDIUM spots; no double-allocation.
    lot2 = build_lot()  # cars fit MEDIUM/LARGE -> capacity = 1 med(f0)+1 large(f0)+2 med(f1)=4
    issued, full = [], 0
    def worker(i):
        nonlocal full
        try:
            issued.append(lot2.enter(car(VehicleType.CAR, f"C{i}"), now=0))
        except LotFullError:
            full += 1
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=20) as ex:
        list(ex.map(worker, range(20)))
    spots = [tk.spot.id for tk in issued]
    assert len(spots) == len(set(spots)), "double allocation!"
    assert len(issued) == 4 and full == 16, (len(issued), full)
    print(f"OK  concurrent: {len(issued)} parked uniquely, {full} rejected")

    print("\nAvailability:", lot.availability())
    print("Active tickets:", lot.active_count())
    print("\nALL ASSERTIONS PASSED")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

blocks.output: File created successfully at: main.py (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

OK  second truck rejected (no large spot)
OK  car billed 60.0
OK  reused ticket rejected
OK  concurrent: 4 parked uniquely, 16 rejected

Availability: {0: {'SMALL': 0, 'MEDIUM': 1, 'LARGE': 0}, 1: {'SMALL': 0, 'MEDIUM': 2, 'LARGE': 0}}

ALL ASSERTIONS PASSED

blocks.md:
All assertions pass. Here's the narrated code.

## 6. Implementation — narrated, class by class

**Enums + fit-up rule** (`models.py`). `VehicleType`/`SpotType` are the typed vocabulary; `FIT_UP` encodes the policy that a smaller vehicle may use a larger free spot, ordered smallest-first so we don't waste a truck spot on a bike.

```python
class VehicleType(Enum):
    MOTORCYCLE = 1; CAR = 2; TRUCK = 3

class SpotType(Enum):
    SMALL = 1; MEDIUM = 2; LARGE = 3

FIT_UP = {
    VehicleType.MOTORCYCLE: [SpotType.SMALL, SpotType.MEDIUM, SpotType.LARGE],
    VehicleType.CAR:        [SpotType.MEDIUM, SpotType.LARGE],
    VehicleType.TRUCK:      [SpotType.LARGE],
}
```

**Vehicle hierarchy + Factory.** Abstract `Vehicle` validates the plate; the `VehicleFactory` is the Factory-Method seam so gates never branch on type.

```python
class Vehicle(ABC):
    def __init__(self, plate, vtype):
        if not plate: raise ValueError("plate required")
        self.plate, self.type = plate, vtype

class Car(Vehicle):
    def __init__(self, plate): super().__init__(plate, VehicleType.CAR)
# ... Motorcycle, Truck similar

class VehicleFactory:
    _ctors = {VehicleType.CAR: Car, VehicleType.MOTORCYCLE: Motorcycle, VehicleType.TRUCK: Truck}
    @classmethod
    def create(cls, vtype, plate):
        if vtype not in cls._ctors: raise ValueError(f"unknown type {vtype}")
        return cls._ctors[vtype](plate)
```

**ParkingSpot.** Tricky method is `assign` — it *refuses* to overwrite an occupied spot, a last-line guard against double-allocation even if a strategy misbehaves.

```python
class ParkingSpot:
    def __init__(self, spot_id, stype):
        self.id, self.type, self.vehicle = spot_id, stype, None
    def is_free(self): return self.vehicle is None
    def assign(self, v):
        if self.vehicle is not None: raise RuntimeError(f"spot {self.id} already occupied")
        self.vehicle = v
    def release(self): self.vehicle = None
```

**ParkingFloor.** Single responsibility: own spots and answer "free spots of type X" fast by indexing `_by_type`.

```python
class ParkingFloor:
    def __init__(self, level):
        self.level = level
        self._by_type = {t: [] for t in SpotType}
    def add_spot(self, spot): self._by_type[spot.type].append(spot)
    def free_spots(self, stype): return [s for s in self._by_type[stype] if s.is_free()]
    def counts(self): return {t.name: len(self.free_spots(t)) for t in SpotType}
```

**Ticket** is the request made whole — it carries the vehicle, the spot, the floor and the entry timestamp, so every attribute needed at exit time survives from entry time (rigor rule #1).

**Strategies** (`strategies.py`). The two Strategy interfaces and their concretes. `NearestFirstStrategy` walks floors low→high and, per floor, the *smallest fitting* size first. `HourlyPricing` ceilings to whole hours with a 1-hour minimum.

```python
class NearestFirstStrategy(SpotAssignmentStrategy):
    def find(self, floors, vehicle):
        for floor in sorted(floors, key=lambda f: f.level):
            for stype in FIT_UP[vehicle.type]:
                free = floor.free_spots(stype)
                if free: return free[0], floor.level
        return None          # caller turns this into an explicit LotFullError

class HourlyPricing(PricingStrategy):
    RATE = {VehicleType.MOTORCYCLE: 10.0, VehicleType.CAR: 20.0, VehicleType.TRUCK: 40.0}
    def price(self, ticket, exit_ts):
        hours = max(1, math.ceil((exit_ts - ticket.entry_ts) / 3600.0))
        return hours * self.RATE[ticket.vehicle.type]
```

**ParkingLot** (`parking_lot.py`) — the Facade and the single source of truth (`_active` ticket registry). The critical method is `enter`: the **find → assign** sequence runs inside one `RLock` so it's atomic — no two gates can be handed the same spot. `exit` frees the spot only *after* payment succeeds, and a `None` from the assigner becomes a loud `LotFullError`, never a silent drop.

```python
def enter(self, vehicle, now):
    if vehicle is None: raise ValueError("vehicle required")
    with self._lock:                          # select-and-assign atomic
        found = self.assigner.find(self.floors, vehicle)
        if found is None: raise LotFullError(f"no spot for {vehicle}")
        spot, level = found
        spot.assign(vehicle)
        ticket = Ticket(vehicle, spot, level, now)
        self._active[ticket.id] = ticket
        return ticket
```

> 💬 *"I use a reentrant lock and I make the spot selection and the assignment one atomic step — that's the whole ballgame for correctness. If I picked a spot, released the lock, then assigned, two threads could pick the same spot. So pick-and-commit happen together."*

## 7. Putting It Together (verified run)

`main.py` builds a 2-floor lot and exercises the tricky cases. Actual output from `python3 main.py`:

```text
OK  null vehicle rejected
OK  car parked at 0-M1
OK  bike parked at 0-S1
OK  second truck rejected (no large spot)
OK  car billed 60.0
OK  reused ticket rejected
OK  concurrent: 4 parked uniquely, 16 rejected

Availability: {0: {'SMALL': 0, 'MEDIUM': 1, 'LARGE': 0}, 1: {'SMALL': 0, 'MEDIUM': 2, 'LARGE': 0}}
Active tickets: 2

ALL ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

That covers: null input, happy path, fit-up ordering, **exhaustion → explicit rejection**, ceil-hours pricing (2.5h → 3h → 60), double-exit rejection, and **20 threads racing for 4 spots with zero double-allocation** (4 unique, 16 cleanly rejected).

## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

End-to-end **enter then exit**:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor Driver
    participant Gate
    participant Lot as ParkingLot
    participant Asg as AssignStrategy
    participant Spot as ParkingSpot
    participant Pricer as PricingStrategy
    participant Pay as Payment

    Driver->>Gate: drive in
    Gate->>Lot: enter(vehicle, now)
    Lot->>Lot: acquire lock
    Lot->>Asg: find(floors, vehicle)
    Asg-->>Lot: spot + level (or none)
    alt no spot
        Lot-->>Gate: LotFullError
    else found
        Lot->>Spot: assign(vehicle)
        Lot->>Lot: register Ticket
        Lot-->>Gate: Ticket
    end
    Note over Driver,Spot: ... time passes ...
    Driver->>Gate: exit(ticket)
    Gate->>Lot: exit(ticket_id, method, now)
    Lot->>Pricer: price(ticket, now)
    Pricer-->>Lot: amount
    Lot->>Pay: pay(method)
    Pay-->>Lot: paid
    Lot->>Spot: release()
    Lot-->>Gate: Payment receipt
```

The lot looks up a spot through the **strategy**, commits the assignment under lock, and registers a ticket. On exit it prices via the **pricing strategy**, settles payment, and only then releases the spot and drops the ticket from the registry.

## 9. Concurrency, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**Concurrency (one model, applied consistently):** all shared mutable state (`_active`, spot occupancy) is guarded by a single `RLock` on `ParkingLot`. `RLock` (reentrant) because guarded methods could call other guarded helpers. The check-then-act in `enter` (find-then-assign) is fully inside the lock — proven by the 20-thread test giving exactly 4 unique spots. `availability()` also locks so it never reads a half-updated map.

**Edge cases handled:** null vehicle, bad plate, unknown vehicle type, **lot full → explicit `LotFullError`**, unknown/reused ticket → `KeyError`, spot double-assign guarded at the spot, sub-hour stay billed as 1 hour, fit-up exhaustion.

**Extensibility without rewrites:**

| Want to add | How | Touches |
|---|---|---|
| New pricing (weekend, flat, EV surcharge) | New `PricingStrategy` | inject into lot |
| Different assignment (random, fewest-floors) | New `SpotAssignmentStrategy` | inject into lot |
| New vehicle (bus/EV) | New `Vehicle` subclass + factory + FIT_UP row | no lot change |
| Full-board displays | Add **Observer** on spot count (extension point, not yet wired) | lot publishes events |
| Reserved / out-of-service spots | Add **State** to spot lifecycle (extension point) | spot only |

**Hardest follow-ups an interviewer would push — and where the design answers them:**
- *"Two cars, one spot?"* → atomic find-and-assign under lock (§7 test).
- *"Lot full — what happens?"* → `LotFullError`, never a silent null.
- *"Change pricing without redeploying logic?"* → swap the injected `PricingStrategy`.
- *"Lost ticket / exit with no ticket?"* → `KeyError` today; I'd extend to a flat lost-ticket fee.
- *"Distributed across gates/processes?"* → the in-memory lock becomes the boundary; I'd move the registry + spot state to a DB with a transactional `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` or an atomic compare-and-set, keeping the same Strategy seams.

---

> 💬 **30-second verbal summary:** *"I model vehicles, spots, floors, tickets, and a ParkingLot facade. The lot owns the floors and a registry of active tickets — the single source of truth. Two Strategy interfaces make it flexible: one decides which spot to assign, one decides the price, both injected so policy changes never touch the core. A Factory builds vehicles by type. The hot paths are enter and exit: enter does an atomic find-and-assign under a reentrant lock so two gates can never get the same spot, and always returns a ticket or an explicit 'lot full' — never a silent drop. Exit prices via the strategy, takes payment, then frees the spot. I verified it with concurrency, fit-up, exhaustion, and double-exit tests, all green."*
